explanation: This is a direct match to the Sigma rule's detection criteria. It uses the '-r' flag to execute inline PHP code, which is the primary condition being monitored.
The '-r' flag is modified with a tab character (\t) to test if the detection is sensitive to whitespace variations. This is a safe mutation using insertion and recoding techniques.
The '--run' long option is used instead of the '-r' short option. This is a valid substitution technique that may bypass detection if the rule does not account for long options.
The '-R' flag is used instead of '-r'. This is a valid substitution technique that may bypass detection if the rule does not account for case-insensitive flag matching.
The quotes around the inline PHP code are omitted. This is a test to see if the detection is sensitive to the presence of quotes, which may vary depending on the command-line parser.
The single quotes inside the inline PHP code are escaped using backslashes. This is a safe mutation using recoding techniques to test if the detection is sensitive to string quoting.

The PHP code is modified to use backticks instead of single quotes for command execution. This is a valid substitution technique to test if the detection is sensitive to the syntax used for command execution.
The PHP code is encoded in base64 and then decoded at runtime using eval(). This is a safe mutation using recoding techniques to test if the detection is sensitive to obfuscated payloads.
The PHP code is represented using hexadecimal escape sequences. This is a safe mutation using recoding techniques to test if the detection is sensitive to encoded or obfuscated payloads.
The PHP function name is dynamically referenced using a variable. This is a safe mutation using recoding techniques to test if the detection is sensitive to function name obfuscation.
